extra.message: Student.create builds the INSERT statement via Python string formatting (`% {'name': name}`) with the user-supplied `name` from the POST handler in sqli/views.py (students view). The formatted string is then executed with cur.execute(q), allowing arbitrary SQL injection via the student name field.

cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

extra.message: Jinja2 is configured with autoescape=False globally. Templates (e.g. course.jinja2 renders {{ review.review_text }}, {{ course.title }}, {{ course.description }}, students.jinja2 renders {{ name }}) output user-supplied data without escaping, enabling stored/reflected XSS. Review text, student name, course title and description are all user-controlled via POST handlers.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The CSRF middleware is commented out in the middleware chain. State-changing POST endpoints (login at /, create student, create course, submit review, evaluate) do not validate any CSRF token, allowing cross-site request forgery.

RedisStorage is created with httponly=False, so the session cookie can be read by JavaScript. Combined with the XSS vulnerabilities (autoescape disabled), this lets an attacker easily steal session cookies.
Passwords are hashed using unsalted MD5. MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsalted hashes are trivial to brute-force with rainbow tables; a leaked pwd_hash exposes user passwords.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: The students handler accepts POST requests that create new Student records without any authentication/authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can create students. Same pattern applies to courses (line 83), review (line 111) and evaluate (line 134) handlers – none are decorated with @authorize().
aiohttp Application is instantiated with debug=True. Debug mode can expose stack traces and internal information to remote users in production.

get_auth_user retrieves user_id from the session without checking if it is present and passes it straight to User.get. If the session has no user_id (unauthenticated request), user_id is None and the SQL query is executed with NULL, returning no user. While this particular query returns None safely, the function never validates user_id type – and if the session cookie is manipulated to include any integer user_id, User.get will load and return that user without verifying credentials, so any valid integer in the session grants access as that user.
